segments.s097-g33: This is an instance of inverted classical word order for emphasis: literally 'one army made Afrasiab.' یکی functions as an indefinite/quantifying preface ('one' / 'a single'), لشکری is a noun used as the direct object ('an army' / 'troops'), ساخت is the past tense verb 'made/raised/assembled,' and افراسیاب is the subject placed after the verb for poetic rhythm.
segments.s097-g34: A prepositional span giving the geographical extent. ز is the preposition 'from' (poetic), دشت 'plain' names the landscape, سپیجاب is a place-name or archaic term modifying دشت, تا is used in the sense 'as far as' / 'up to,' and رود آب is a compound nominal phrase 'the river' (رود 'river' + آب 'water' used appositively). The whole phrase specifies the territory covered by the raised army.
segments.s097-g35: که introduces an indirect statement ('that' / 'which was said'). گفتی here functions as a nominalized report ('the saying' / 'it was said'), زمین is the subject 'the earth,' شد is the past of 'become,' and سپهر روان is a noun plus postpositive adjective: سپهر 'sky/heaven' modified by روان 'moving/flowing.' The clause means roughly 'it was said that the earth became a flowing sky.'
segments.s097-g36: همی is an archaic adverb meaning 'continually' or 'kept on'; بارد here is an older/poetic verbal form meaning 'falls/keeps falling' (not the modern adjective 'cold') and functions as the intransitive verb; از marks source ('from'), تیغ is the noun 'sword,' هندی is the adjective 'Indian' modifying تیغ, and روان postmodifies تیغ as 'swift/flowing.' Together: 'Indian swords kept raining down.'
segments.s097-g37: یکایک is an adverb meaning 'one by one' or 'one after another;' به is the preposition 'to/toward' marking the destination, ایران is the target place, رسید is the past tense verb 'reached/arrived,' and آگهی is the noun 'news/information' serving as the subject/theme. The adverb fronted gives the manner in which the news arrived.
segments.s097-g38: که introduces the content of the news ('that'). آمد is the past verb 'came/arrived,' خریدار is 'buyer/purchaser' (head noun), تخت مهی is a genitival noun phrase 'the lofty/beautiful throne' (تخت 'throne' modified by مهی 'moon‑like/beautiful'), so خریدار تخت مهی is 'the purchaser of the majestic throne.' The clause reports that such a buyer had come.
segments.s097-g39: سوی 'toward' marks direction; زابلستان is the place name (Zabulistan); نهادند is the past 3rd‑person plural of نهادن 'to place/set,' and together with روی (face) forms the idiom روی نهادن 'to set one's face toward / to turn toward.' Word order here places the directional phrase first for emphasis: 'Toward Zabulistan they set their faces.'
segments.s097-g40: جهان is the grammatical subject 'the world,' شد is the past 'became,' سراسر is an adverb/adjective 'throughout/entire,' پر 'full (of)' combines with از to mark content, and گفت‌وگوی (the construct form of گفت‌وگو) is the noun 'conversation/talk.' The clause therefore means 'the world became entirely full of talk.'
segments.s097-g57: کنون is an adverb placed frontally ('now'). چنبری is a predicate-nominal meaning 'ring/hoop' and گشت (past of گشتن used as 'became') follows it; the phrase یال یلی ('mane mighty') names what changed — an appositive/nominal phrase where the adjective-like یلی follows یال without an ezāfe (typical in classical/poetic Persian).
segments.s097-g58: نتابد is the negative present-form of تابیدن here meaning 'does not bend/turn' (impersonal/resultative sense); همی is an emphatic/continuous particle (here strengthening the negation). خنجر کابلی is the noun phrase 'the Kabuli dagger' (agent), and the object of 'bend' is omitted but picked up from the previous segment (the hoop-like mane).
segments.s097-g59: کنون 'now' introduces the clause. گشت 'became' predicates about رستم. چو + سرو سهی is a simile 'like a dark cypress' where سهی ('dark/black') follows سرو without an ezāfe, a poetic adjective placement.
segments.s097-g60: بزیبد here is impersonal/psychological: 'it befits / it becomes him'. The following verbal/locative phrase 'برو بر کلاه مهی' expresses what befits him — literally 'to go on/put upon the moon-like cap' and so naturally translated 'to don the moon-like cap'; برو functions as part of that infinitival/oblique verbal phrase, and مهی is a descriptive adjective 'moon-like/beautiful' modifying کلاه.
segments.s097-g61: یکی is the numeral/article 'one'. اسپ جنگیش is a compound sense 'war-horse' (جنگی as an attributive of جنگ). باید is the modal 'must/is necessary'. همی functions as an emphatic/continuative particle reinforcing the necessity.
segments.s097-g62: کزین 'from these' introduces the reason/contrast. تازی اسپان is the noun phrase 'Tazi (Arabian) horses' with تازی qualifying اسپان (no ezāfe in poetic diction). نشاید is impersonal 'it is not fitting/it will not do', and همی is an emphatic particle reiterating that unsuitability.
segments.s097-g63: بجویم is a present/subjunctive-first-person form 'I will seek / I shall look for'. یکی باره is an adverbial phrase 'one time / once' modifying the seeking, and پیلتن is the place-name object (Pilten) of the verb.
segments.s097-g64: بخواهم is first-person 'I will / I shall (ask/attempt)'. ز هر سو 'from every side' is a prepositional phrase indicating source/direction. که هست انجمن is a relative/subordinate clause 'that/where the assembly is' (classical ordering places که before the verb), giving the content of the inquiry.
segments.s097-g9: This clause uses the impersonal past construction خبر شد (literally "news happened") to mean "news reached/it became known." The preposition به marks the experiencer/indirect object (به ترکان = "to the Turks"). The phrase که زو درگذشت is a subordinate clause introduced by که ("that") with ز (poetic form of از) + و (here representing the clitic pronoun 'him') and the past verb درگذشت ("passed away/died").
segments.s097-g10: بران سان که introduces a consequence/result clause: بران سان (thereupon/thereon) + سان ("like/so") together mean "in such a way/thereupon"; که again introduces the content of the result. بد تخت بی‌کار گشت is a verbal phrase where بد modifies تخت ("throne") and گشت is the past of گشتن used as "became" — so بی‌کار ("unoccupied/idle") is the resultant state of the throne.
segments.s097-g11: بیامد is the simple past third‑person singular "came." The prepositional phrase به خوار ری gives the destination (to Khwar and Rey); word order is verb first (poetic inversion) with the place following. خوار here functions as a place-name in context (not the lexical senses offered), while ری is the city Rey; افراسیاب is the overt subject/naming after the destination.
segments.s097-g12: ببخشید is a past‑tense form of بخشیدن used here in a poetic register (3rd person past). It takes گیتی ("the world/earth") as its object. The conjunction و links the following clause; بگذاشت is past 3rd singular of گذاشتن with the prosthetic ب‑ (poetic) meaning "he left/let/allowed," and آب is its object. Overall the two verbs report two successive actions by the same subject.
segments.s097-g13: نیاورد is the negative past (did not bring). The noun phrase یک تن ("a single person") is the syntactic object quantified by یک, followed by درود ("greeting") and the proper name پشنگ (Pashang) as the beneficiary/target: literally "did not bring one person’s greeting to Pashang" — idiomatically "not one person came to greet Pashang."
segments.s097-g14: This is a coordinate descriptive clause: سرش پر ز کین بود — سرش (his head) + پر ز ("full of") + کین ("hatred/vengeance") + بود (past of بودن). The second half mirrors the first with و (and) introducing دل پر ز جنگ (his heart full of war). Note the possessor clitic ش after سر and دل.
segments.s097-g15: دلش خود ز تخت و کله گشته بود is a past perfect‑like description: دلش (his heart) + خود (emphatic 'itself') + ز (poetic 'from/of') تخت و کله ("throne and head") + گشته بود (the compound past of گشتن: 'had become/turned'). Word order is emphatic and somewhat inverted for poetic effect; گشته (perfect participle/turn‑become) combines with بود to give the past‑state meaning.
segments.s097-g16: به تیمار اغریرث آغشته بود is a copular description: به تیمار ("in/to the care/care of") + اغریرث (a poetic/figurative name) + آغشته (adjective 'soaked/imbued') + بود (past 'was'). The preposition به introduces the state, and آغشته بود expresses that he was saturated/imbued with that concern or influence.
segments.s097-g25: This is a negative existential statement: the predicate کار نیست ('there is no affair/dealing') closes the clause. The phrase مرا با تو ('me with you') places the two participants in relation (مرا here is the object 'me', formed from م + را), and تا جاودان ('until/for ever') modifies کار to mean 'a lasting/eternal business'. The object marker را is attached in the surface tokens as a separate item (م + را = مرا).
segments.s097-g26: به نزد منت is a prepositional phrase meaning 'to/in the presence of (your) favor' (به + نزد = 'to/at'; نزد here = 'in the presence of'). راه دیدار ('the way/path of meeting') is the noun phrase governed by the copula نیست 'does not exist', so the whole clause says there is no access/means for a meeting in that presence.
segments.s097-g27: پرآواز شد is a verbal phrase 'became loud/resounding' (شد = 'became'). The noun گوش ('ear') is the affected noun; word order places the predicate (پرآواز شد) before its patient (گوش), a poetic inversion. ازین آگهی ('from this news') is a source phrase (از + این) giving the cause/source of the sound/attention.
segments.s097-g28: The conjunction/complementizer که introduces the content of the news: بی‌کار شد ('became idle/vacant') is a compound where بی‌کار is an adjective 'vacant/idle' and شد is the past/copular verb 'became'. تخت شاهنشهی is a genitival/nominal phrase 'the imperial throne' (تخت 'throne' qualified by شاهنشهی 'imperial').
segments.s097-g29: پیامی بیامد is a simple past-event clause 'a message came' (پیامی indefinite + بیامد 'came'). The following phrase به کردار سنگ uses به with an abstract noun کردار ('manner') plus سنگ ('stone') as a simile: 'in the manner of a stone' / 'delivered like a stone' — here به is functioning adverbially ('in the way of').
segments.s097-g30: This short prepositional cluster marks recipient and source: به افراسیاب ('to Afrasiab' — به as dative/recipient) and از دلاور پشنگ ('from the valiant Pashang' — از marking origin). دلاور is an epithet modifying the proper name پشنگ.
segments.s097-g31: که introduces the reported command/clause. بگذار is a jussive/imperative ('let [it/them] be' / 'let go / set') and takes the coordinated names جیحون و برکش plus the head noun سپاه, so the phrase means 'let (the) armies of Jihun and Barkash [be/march]'. The coordination (X و Y) precedes سپاه, a common order for naming multiple forces/places followed by a collective noun.
segments.s097-g32: ممان here is an imperative/command meaning 'do not withhold/hinder' (negative instruction to refrain from obstructing). تا introduces an until-clause: کسی برنشیند به گاه — برنشیند is a subjunctive/potential verb 'may sit / take a seat', and به گاه 'at the time / at the appointed moment' gives the temporal limit.
segments.s097-g65: بخوانم is the first-person present/imperfect form with the present-prefix ب‑ + خوان (read/recite) + ‑م, here expressing the speaker's intention “I will recite/relate.” به رستم is the prepositional phrase “to/for Rostam” (به = to/for, رستم = the name). بر این داستان literally “on/over this tale” means “this tale/about this tale” and places the following clause as its content.
segments.s097-g66: که introduces a content clause “that …”. هستی here is the verb-form meaning “you are” (homograph of the noun 'existence'); in classical/epic Persian this form often stands for 2nd-person present. بر…کار = برین کار (here written as بر + ین + کار) means “about/regarding this affair” (بر = on/about; ین is the older/poetic marker related to این ‘this’; کار = affair/matter). همداستان is an adjective meaning “of the same mind / in agreement” and predicates the clause.
segments.s097-g67: که again introduces a subordinate clause “that …”. بر کینه = “out of/for hatred” (بر used here with a sense close to ‘because of/against’). تخمه زادشم is a genitival phrase: تخمه = ‘seed/offspring’ and زادشم is a proper name (Zādsham) qualifying that offspring.
segments.s097-g68: ببندی is the imperfect/subjunctive/optative form (prefix ب‑ + بند ‘bind/put’ + ‑ی) functioning as a 2nd/3rd‑person verbal form here — “you place/bind” or “may you place.” میان = “between.” و introduces the next clause. نباشی is the negative present/subjunctive/imperative (نباش + ‑ی) meaning “do not be / may you not be.” دژم is an adjective meaning “sullen/grim,” the predicate of the negative clause.
segments.s097-g69: همه شهر ایران = “all the cities/towns of Iran” (همه = all, شهر = city/town, ایران = Iran). ز گفتار اوی is a causal/agentive phrase “from/because of his speech” (ز = from/because of; گفتار = speech/utterance; اوی = his/her — here “his” referring back to the named person).
segments.s097-g70: ببودند is the past plural copular form (“they were”); it places the description in the past. شادان دل = “glad of heart” (شادان = joyful, دل = heart) and و تازه روی = “and fresh‑faced” (تازه = fresh, روی = face), both predicates describing the towns/people as affected by his speech.
segments.s097-g71: ز هر سو = “from every direction/side” (ز = from; هر = every; سو = side/direction). هیونی تکاور pairs two nouns/adjectives: هیونی (beastly/savage creatures) qualifies تکاور (raider/elite rider), and بتاخت is the past form with prefix ب‑ of تختن/تاختن meaning “he/she/they galloped/charged” — the clause describes a charge from all sides.
segments.s097-g72: سلیح سواران جنگی is a noun phrase meaning “armed war‑horsemen” (سلیح as adjective ‘armed’/relating to weapons, سواران = horsemen/riders, جنگی = warlike/military). بساخت here is a past verbal form (ب‑ + ساخت) meaning “he/they made/raised/arrayed,” i.e., formed or put into being these armed riders; the printed sense list did not match this verbal sense exactly, so the gloss reflects its syntactic/semantic role as a past action.
segments.s097-g73: به رستم چنین گفت is a typical classical Persian order: the indirect object/goal (به رستم, 'to Rostam') comes before the verb گفت ('said'); چنین ('thus') is an adverb modifying گفت. The verb گفت is simple past; what follows (کای پیلتن) is a vocative address introduced by کای.
segments.s097-g74: به بالا سرت برتر از انجمن is a compact predication with an omitted copula (است) typical of poetry. به بالا + سرت gives the locative/position 'over/above your head', برتر از introduces the comparative 'higher/superior than', and انجمن is the object of comparison. Word order places the locative phrase before the possessed noun (سرت) and the comparative phrase at the end.
segments.s097-g75: یکی کار پیشست و رنجی دراز: یکی کار ('a certain task') is followed by the verb پیشست ('lies before/is ahead') — in older Persian the predicate follows the noun. The conjunction و links the next nominal phrase رنجی دراز ('a long suffering') in apposition or as a second item of predication.
segments.s097-g76: کزو بگسلد خواب و آرام و ناز: کزو ('from which / of which') is a relative/adverbial referring back to رنجی دراز; بگسلد is a classical present/future-form verb (ب‑ + گسَلَد) meaning 'will break/tear apart'. The verb takes the coordinated objects خواب و آرام و ناز ('sleep, rest, and indulgence'), listed with و.
segments.s097-g77: ترا (here represented as ت + را) marks the second person ('to/for you'); نوز پورا ('completely newborn/utterly new') is a predicative phrase describing the addressee. گه رزم نیست ('the occasion/time for battle does not exist') puts گه ('occasion/time') + رزم ('battle') before the existential negation نیست; the copular/existential sense is expressed by نیست, common in classical Persian.
segments.s097-g78: چه سازم که هنگامه بزم نیست is a rhetorically framed complaint: چه سازم ('what shall I do' — سازم is 1sg present/subjunctive 'I make/do') followed by که introducing the reason/explanation ('since/because'); هنگامه بزم نیست states that there is no occasion for revelry. The order places the rhetorical question first and the explanatory clause after که.
segments.s097-g79: هنوز از لبت شیر بوید همی: هنوز ('still/even now') is an adverb of time; از لبت ('from your lip' = on your lip) gives the source; شیر ('milk') is the subject and بوید ('emits/smells') is a present form meaning 'gives off' or 'is of the smell/taste' — here idiomatically 'milk is on/at your lips'. همی is a classical continuous/habitual marker reinforcing ongoing action.
segments.s097-g80: دلت ناز و شادی بجوید همی: دلت ('your heart' — دل + 2nd-person suffix ت) is the subject; ناز و شادی ('affection/caresses and joy') are coordinated objects of بجوید ('seeks/longs for'), with بجوید in the present habitual. The final همی again marks ongoing/habitual action, so the whole clause means 'your heart still seeks tenderness and joy.'
segments.s097-g1: This clause states existence/possession: پسر بود 'there was a son'. Classical Persian often puts the copula بود after the noun. ز + و here form زُو = 'of him' (a poetic genitive), followed by the object/topic marker را for emphasis. یکی خویِ ش کام literally 'a certain (one) of his own-disposition's desire' — i.e. 'a certain one who fulfilled his (the father's) desire.' Word order is somewhat compressed/archaic: 'یکی' ('a certain') qualifies the following noun phrase 'خویِ‌ش کام'.
segments.s097-g2: پدر is agent/actor 'the father'. کرده is the past participle/verb 'made' (کرده بود = 'had made'); بودیش is an older/contracted form meaning roughly 'had been to/for him' or 'it belonged to him' in sense of past perfect auxiliary + clitic; together they express 'his father had made him' or 'the father had so made (him)'. The final two words give the name: گرشاسپ نام = 'his name [was] Garshasp.'
segments.s097-g3: بیامد نشست is a two-part action: بیامد 'came' followed by نشست 'sat' (verb). از بر تخت is a classical locative string meaning 'upon the throne' (از بر = on/upon + تخت = throne). و گاه ties to the next clause: 'and sometimes.'
segments.s097-g4: به سر بر نهاد is 'placed upon the head': به = 'to/on', سر = 'head', بر نهاد = past of نهادن 'placed/put on'. آن کیانی کلاه is the object 'that Kayanian hat/crown' (کیانی = Kayanian/royal). The verb precedes the emphasized object phrase (typical flexible classical order).
segments.s097-g5: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بنشست is the past/preterite verb 'he sat down' (here marking habitual or aone-time action in narrative). بر تخت 'upon the throne'. و گاه 'and sometimes' introduces the manner/frequency; پدر in this line is vocative/epithet 'father (of the world)' that continues into the next segment (see next segment).
segments.s097-g6: جهان را is the object/topic 'the world' marked by را. همی داشت is an ongoing/habitual past: همی (classical continuous/iterative marker) + داشت 'held/possessed'. با زیب و فر 'with beauty and far/royal glory' specifies the manner — فر here is the technical epic term for royal splendour. The verb داشت takes the following instrument phrase with با.
segments.s097-g7: چنین 'thus/so' sets the narrative tone. تا here means 'until', and برآمد 'arose/appeared' is the verbal pivot. برین is the fused preposition/ demonstrative 'بر + این' = 'this' (classical spelling ین as a variant of این), so برین روزگار = 'this time/period' or 'this tide of events.'
segments.s097-g8: درخت بلا is a metaphorical genitive 'the tree of calamity' (درخت = tree, بلا = calamity). کینه آورد بار: آورد 'brought/caused' and بار 'burden/load' — the clause says the calamity-tree brought/borne a load of hatred (i.e. hatred was produced/as a burden). Word order places the object/compliment after the verb, as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s097-g81: چگونه is an interrogative adverb 'how'. فرستم is first-person singular present-subjunctive/potential ('should I send / how can I send'), with the subject 'I' understood. به دشت نبرد is a prepositional phrase headed by به ('to/towards') followed by the compound noun دشت نبرد ('plain of battle'), functioning as the destination of فرستم.
segments.s097-g82: ترا is the direct object 'you' built from the enclitic ت plus the object marker را (object definite marker); it appears before the following locative phrase. پیش ترکان is a locative/prepositional phrase meaning 'in the presence of / before the Turks' (پیش + plural ترکان). پر is an adjectival premodifier meaning 'full (of)', followed by the nouns کین و درد in coordination ('hatred and sorrow/affliction') describing the Turks.
segments.s097-g83: This is a triplet of interrogative phrases built the same way: each چه is the interrogative 'what', followed by a second-person present/subjunctive verb form addressing the listener. گویی here functions as 'you say' (present/subjunctive), سازی is 'you will make/devise' (or 'what means will you use'), and پاسخ‌دهی is 'you will answer' (or 'what reply will you give'); they are coordinated questions directed to the addressee.
segments.s097-g84: که introduces the following wish/intention clause. جفت تو means 'your mate/partner' (جفت = pair/spouse) with تو 'your'. بادا is an optative particle 'may it be' (a wish). مهی و بهی are two praising adjectives joined by و: مهی 'moon‑like / beautiful', بهی 'good/fair/excellent' — together forming the blessing 'may your partner be moonlike and fair.'
segments.s097-g85: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' setting the manner of speech. گفت is the past tense verb 'he said' (3rd person past). رستم is the speaker's name. به دستان سام is a prepositional phrase 'to the hands of Sam' — idiomatically 'to Sam' or 'addressed to Sam' (به + دستان used here to indicate the person addressed).
segments.s097-g86: که introduces reported speech 'that'. من نیستم is the negated copula 'I am not' (نیستم = negative present of بودن). مرد آرام و جام is the predicative noun phrase: مرد 'a man' qualified by آرام 'peaceful/quiet' and جام 'the cup/wine' (here 'man of ease and cup' = a man given to repose and drinking), so the clause means 'I am not a man given to quietness and drinking.'
segments.s097-g87: This is a coordinated nominal phrase: چنین 'such' modifies یال 'a mane', و 'and' links to این 'these' + چنگهای دراز 'long claws' (چنگهای here means 'claws/talons'). The phrase enumerates the animal's magnificent features ('such a mane and these long claws').
segments.s097-g88: The clause has an inverted, literary word order: نه (negation) + والا 'noble/fit' + بود 'was' (past copula) + پروریدن (verbal noun 'to rear/nurture') + به ناز ('with pampering/indulgence'). Literally: 'To rear with pampering was not noble,' i.e. these features were not meant to be raised in delicate indulgence. Note the gerundial noun پروریدن used as the subject of بود.
segments.s097-g17: بدو (bedo) is a prepositional/locative clitic meaning 'to him' (toward him) placed before the object; روی (rūy) is the noun 'face' and ننمود (nannamūd) is an archaic past-tense verb 'did not show' (نمود with negative prefix نن-). هرگز (hargez) 'never' emphasizes the negation; پشنگ (Pashang) is the proper name and the subject. Word order is object/locative + noun + verb + negator + adverb + subject in the terse, poetic style.
segments.s097-g18: شد (shod) is the simple past 'became' or 'was made'; آن (ān) is the demonstrative 'that/the'; تیغ (tīgh) 'sword' is the subject; روشن (rowshan) 'bright' is an adjective modifying تیغ. پر از (por az) is the idiom 'full of'; تیره (tīreh) 'dark' modifies زنگ (zang) 'rust' here. The clause follows a Subject-Predicate order typical of Persian poetry with a copular/resultative شد.
segments.s097-g19: فرستاده (ferestāde) 'the messenger' is the subject. رفتی (rafti) here functions as a past tense 'went' with an archaic verbal ending (poetic past), followed by به نزدیک اوی 'to near him'—به 'to/toward', نزدیک 'near', اوی an old form of او 'him'. The clause is straightforward: subject + verb + prepositional phrase.
segments.s097-g20: بدو (bedo) again is 'to him'; سال و مه literally 'year and moon/month' is a fixed temporal expression meaning 'for a year and a month' (i.e., a long time). هیچ ننمود روی: هیچ 'no/none' (here negating the gesture), ننمود 'did not show', روی 'face' — together 'he showed him no face at all.' Note the verb appears after the temporal phrase, typical in poetic inversion.
segments.s097-g21: همی گفت (hamī goft) is a habitual or iterative past meaning 'he used to say' or 'he kept saying' (همی marking continuity). اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. تخت را is the definite object 'the throne' (را marks the object). سر here is 'leadership/top/head' (سر تخت = the leadership/seat of the throne) and بدی is used as a predicate meaning 'you do wrong' or 'you commit harm' in this context (an older/poetic form; see next segment for parallelism). The clause is a conditional protasis introduced in direct speech.
segments.s097-g22: چو (cho) here = 'when/if/as' linking the two parts of the condition. اغریرثش is the name Aghrirath with the enclitic possessive -ش; یار 'companion/ally' names the role to be given; درخور 'worthy/appropriate' modifies یار; بدی repeats the moral/ethical predicate 'you do wrong' or 'you commit an evil' — a parallel structure (if you do X and make Y, you will be doing wrong). The sentence uses poetic economy and an archaic flavor in verbal/noun choices.
segments.s097-g23: تو (to) 'you' is the subject; خون (khun) 'blood' and برادر (barādar) 'brother' form an object phrase 'the blood of (your) brother' (object relationship is implicit). بریزی (berīzi) is an archaic/poetic present/future-like form 'you will pour/shed' (from ریختن/ریخت); همی marks habitual or emphatic aspect 'indeed/altogether'. The clause is a straightforward prediction/accusation addressed to 'you'.
segments.s097-g24: ز (z) 'from' (poetic variant of از) introduces the source/cause; پرورده 'nurtured, reared' modifies مرغی 'bird' — together 'a reared/tame bird'. گریزی (gerīzi) is the verb 'you will flee/escape' (poetic/future sense), and همی again gives emphasis. The contrast (killing your brother but fleeing from a tame bird) is a moral rebuke: bravery in the wrong place and cowardice in another.
segments.s097-g49: چنین گفت پس is a short reporting formula: چنین 'thus', گفت (past 3rd sing.) 'said' (classical past), پس 'then/afterwards'. نامور زال زر is a nominal phrase in apposition: نامور 'renowned' modifying the name زال, with زر an epithet ('gold'/'golden') placed after the name (poetic apposition).
segments.s097-g50: که تا introduces a subordinate temporal clause: که ... تا = 'that / when' or 'so that / until' (classical usage where تا often = 'when/as soon as'). من is the subject. ببستم is an archaic/perfective past form of بستن with the prefixed be- (classical poetic morphology) meaning 'I girded/tied'; the verb naturally takes the object کمر 'waist / girdle'. به مردی کمر بستن is an idiomatic/constructed phrase 'to gird the belt to a man' = 'to gird oneself as a man / to take a husband or to assume a man's girdle' — کمر here denotes the belt/girdle.
segments.s097-g51: سواری 'a rider/horseman' is the topical noun; چو من is a comparative/temporal 'like/when I' (چو here = 'when/as'); پای بر زین نگاشت is a typical classical sequence meaning 'placed (his) foot on the saddle' — پای 'foot', بر 'on/upon', زین 'saddle'. نگاشت is a past (3rd sg.) classical form meaning 'placed/put' (classical poetic diction).
segments.s097-g52: کسی here functions as an indefinite with negative sense in context ('no one / no person'). تیغ و گرز is the coordinated object 'sword and mace'. م + را is the clitic sequence marking the object with possession/emphasis ('...my ...' or '... from me' in older usage): م = object suffix 'me / my', را = definite/object marker. برنداشت is past (3rd sg.) composed verb with بر- prefix meaning 'lifted / took away' — so the clause says 'no one took away my sword and mace.'
segments.s097-g53: به جایی که is a locative subordinating phrase 'to the place that / to the very spot where'. من is subject. پای بفشاردم is a classical verbal clause: پای 'foot' + بفشاردم (1st sg. past with prefixed be-) from فشاریدن 'to press', i.e. 'I pressed (down) my foot' — be- prefix is a typical classical/conservative past formation.
segments.s097-g54: عنان سواران is a genitival noun phrase 'the reins of the horsemen' (عنان head noun + سواران 'riders' as possessor). شدی is past (3rd sg.) of شدن 'became / came to be' used here as a resultative auxiliary. پاردم is an adverbial/time/poetic particle ('once', 'formerly', or 'thereupon' in Ferdowsi's diction) that qualifies the resulting action; together the sequence describes a consequential change affecting the riders' reins.
segments.s097-g55: شب و روز 'night and day' is a fixed coordinate expression. در جنگ is a locative phrase 'in battle / in warfare'. یکسان modifies the following predicate to mean 'the same / equal / uniformly'. بدم is a 1st‑person predicate in Ferdowsi's poetic register (here functioning as 'I was / I acted' in the past sense); together the clause says 'day and night in battle I was the same' (i.e. equally active/unchanging).
segments.s097-g56: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'. پیری 'old age' is the cause; همه‌ساله 'year by year / every year' is an adverbial modifier. ترسان is an adjectival/participial form meaning 'frightened / made fearful' or 'fearful' describing the speaker's state. بدم repeats the same 1st‑person predicate form as above, so the clause reads 'from old age, year by year I became fearful.'
segments.s097-g41: بگفتند is a past plural verb 'they said' (active voice). با زال is a prepositional phrase 'with/to Zāl' (در classical usage با can mark the addressee). چندی درشت is an adverbial phrase modifying the manner of speaking: چندی 'somewhat' and درشت 'coarse/rough', i.e. 'somewhat rude/harsh'. Word order places the verb up front in this poetic line and follows it with the dative/recipient phrase and the adverbial manner phrase.
segments.s097-g42: که introduces reported speech ('that'/'saying that'). گیتی is the direct object 'the world'. بس here is an emphatic/intensifying particle ('enough' → 'very/too'), آسان is an adjective 'easy', and گرفتی is the 2nd‑person singular past 'you seized/took'. به مشت is a locative/instrumental phrase 'into/within the fist' (به = towards/into + مشت 'fist'). The clause reports what they told Zāl.
segments.s097-g43: پس از سام تا ... is a temporal frame: پس از سام 'after/from Sām' and تا introduces the endpoint 'until'. تو شدی پهلوان is a past clause 'you became a champion' (تو = you; شدی = became). The sequence marks a span of time from the era of Sām up to the addressee's rise as a hero.
segments.s097-g44: نبودیم is the negative past plural 'we were not / we did not have' (here idiomatically 'we did not see/experience'). یک روز روشن‌روان follows as the negated quantity: 'a single bright‑souled day' (روز = day; روشن‌روان a compound adjective meaning cheerful/bright). Persian here omits a verb like 'داشتیم' and uses نبودیم to express 'we did not have/experience'.
segments.s097-g45: سپاهی is a collective noun 'an army/force'. ز جیحون is the source phrase 'from the Jeyhun' (ز = from; جیحون = the Amu Darya). بدین سو is a directional phrase 'to this side' (بدین = 'to this' + سو = 'side/toward'). کشید is the past tense of کشیدن and here means 'marched/moved/advanced' (poetic usage). The full segment describes the army's movement.
segments.s097-g46: که introduces the result/clause of consequence 'so that'. شد is the past 'became' (here auxiliary sense 'came to be'), آفتاب = 'the sun', از جهان = 'from the world' (از = from), and ناپدید = 'vanished/was lost'. The clause states the dramatic consequence of the army's advance.
segments.s097-g47: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. چاره دانی is 'if you know a remedy' (چاره = remedy/solution; دانی = you know/are able to find). مراین را is the object 'for this' — مراین is an archaic/poetic demonstrative/compound meaning 'this matter' and را is the definite/object marker. بساز is the 2nd‑person singular imperative 'make/devise'. Together: 'If you know a solution to this, make it.'
segments.s097-g48: که here gives the reason 'for/because': 'for the spahbed came ...'. آمد is past 'came/has come', سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (general). به تنگی فراز is a prepositional phrase describing the condition arrived in: به تنگی 'into narrowness/distress' (تنگی = hardship/distress) and فراز 'up/on high' or 'the summit' used idiomatically; the whole means 'has come into a tight/desperate situation.'
segments.s097-g97: یکی باره is an adverbial phrase meaning 'once / at one time' (literal 'one occasion'). باید is the modal 'must/ought' in literary Persian; چو + noun phrase (چو کوه بلند) introduces a simile 'like a tall mountain'. Word order: temporal adverb → modal → simile phrase; the clause is incomplete until the main verb appears in the next segment.
segments.s097-g98: چنان چون is an emphatic double simile meaning 'so / just as'; من آرم is a first‑person present/subjunctive-like form 'I rest / I lie (may I lie)', completing the predicate required by باید. به خم کمند is a prepositional phrase 'into/in the bend of the snare' (به = toward/into, خم = curve, کمند = lasso/snare). Verb placement (آرم) follows the simile construction in this older poetic diction.
segments.s097-g99: یکی گرز خواهم: یکی 'one/a single' modifies the object گرز 'mace', and خواهـم is the first‑person auxiliary meaning 'I will / I want' (future/volitional sense) — 'I will take a mace.' چو یک لخت کوه is a simile 'like a single bare mountain' modifying the manner in which he takes the mace. Object + auxiliary + simile ordering reflects poetic syntax.
segments.s097-g100: گرآیند is a third‑person plural classical/subjunctive form meaning 'they come' or 'should come' (here describing an imagined/anticipated troop). پیشم 'before me / in my presence' follows the verb; ز توران گروه is an agent phrase 'a troop from Turan' with ز as poetic 'from' and گروه 'troop/band'. The clause is conditional/imagined rather than a strict modern indicative.
segments.s097-g101: سرانشان 'their leaders/their heads' is the object. بکوبم is a first‑person poetic form of 'to strike / to smite' (ب‑ prefix + کوبم) meaning 'I strike / I will strike.' بدان گرز is an archaic phrasing equivalent to 'با آن گرز' ('with that mace'): here the elements are split as ب + دان + گرز in the manuscript; بر at the end functions as a prepositional/relational particle ('upon/against') linking to the next clause. This line shows older, concatenated forms and a compact word order.
segments.s097-g102: نیاید is a negative classical form 'does not come / will not come' (here with jussive/anticipatory sense). برم is the clitic/element meaning 'upon me' (بر + ـم), so نیاید برم = 'shall not come upon me.' هیچ 'no/none' negates the following noun, and پرخاشخر is a noun meaning 'a quarrelsome/hostile person'. The clause therefore means 'no quarrelsome man will come against me.'
segments.s097-g103: که introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / in order that'). روی زمین را is a definite object 'the face/surface of the earth' (روی = face/surface, را = definite object marker). کنم is first‑person singular 'I make / I cause,' and بی‌سپاه is a predicative phrase 'without an army.' The clause means 'so that I make the earth's surface without troops.'
segments.s097-g104: The second که again links a further result/clause ('that/so that'). خون is 'blood'; ابر 'cloud' and اندر 'in/within' combine with آوردگاه 'battlefield.' The verb sense for بارد in the manuscript would be 'to fall / to rain' (i.e., 'to pour down'), but the provided senses list gives only adjectival 'cold' forms and thus does not match the verbal use here (marked below). The whole clause reads 'that clouds may pour blood upon the battlefield.'
segments.s097-g89: This is a prototypical conditional clause introduced by اگر (if). دشت کین is a noun + modifier construction (دشتِ کین = 'plain of vengeance') with the verb آید (3rd‑sg. 'comes' or 'appears') following the noun phrase, then coordinating و 'and' links the next nominal phrase رزم سخت ('battle/semi‑predicate + adjective'). The adjective سخت modifies رزم; word order (N V / N Adj) is typical of classical Persian clauses.
segments.s097-g90: بود is the copula (‘is/was’) linking the subject phrase (implied or topical) to the predicate that follows: یار یزدان پیروزبخت is a nominal phrase meaning 'the ally (companion/ally) blessed/fortunate by God'. In epic Persian بود often functions as a simple present/past copula; here it links the condition to the assertion about the ally.
segments.s097-g91: ببینی is the 2nd‑person form (subjunctive/optative/imperative flavor) meaning 'you will see' or 'you shall see' in narrative style; که introduces the subordinate clause. در جنگ من is a locative phrase 'in my battle' (literally 'in battle of me' — my conduct in battle). چون شوم is a subordinated clause 'how I become' (چون = 'how/as', شوم = 1st‑sg. subjunctive/optative 'I become').
segments.s097-g92: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when/as'; اندر پی is a fixed‑feeling prepositional sequence (اندر = 'in/within', پی = 'after/in the wake of'), so اندر پی ریزش خون reads 'when/at the onset of the falling/flowing of blood'. شوم repeats the 1st‑sg. subjunctive 'I become' and completes the temporal subordinate clause. The clause is vivid/prophetic in mood (subjunctive used for dramatic effect).
segments.s097-g93: یکی is an indefinite 'one/a certain'; ابر is the noun 'cloud' and دارم is the 1st‑person present 'I have/possess'. به + چنگ indicates location/means 'in/at the grasp' (به چنگ = 'in the clench/at hand'), and اندرون ('inside/within') completes the phrase — literal order 'one cloud I have in the inner grasp'.
segments.s097-g94: که introduces a relative clause modifying the cloud. همرنگ is a compound adjective 'same‑colored/as the color of', and آبست is an older/poetic form meaning 'is watery/filled with water' (here 'has the color/quality of water'). و links the second predication 'بارانش خون' = 'its rain is blood' (باران + possessive suffix ش). The relative clause gives two attributes of the cloud.
segments.s097-g95: همی is an adverb of continuous or emphatic aspect ('continually/always') placed before the verb. آتش is the object 'fire' and افروزد is the 3rd‑person present/epic form of افروختن 'to kindle/ignite'; از گوهرش is a prepositional phrase 'from its essence/core' with the possessive suffix -ش. The order (adverb + object + verb + از + possessed noun) is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s097-g96: Again همی marks repeated/continuous action. مغز پیلان is a genitive construction 'the brains of the elephants' (مغز = 'brain', پیلان = plural/collective of 'elephant'). بساید is the 3rd‑person form of an older/poetic verb (here used actively) meaning roughly 'gnaws/wears away/acts upon' — an archaic sense appropriate to epic diction. سرش is 'their/its head' (سر + possessive -ش); the structure places the affected parts (brains, head) next to the verb they undergo.

sentences.s097-s17: Afrasiab raised an army from the plain of Sepyjab as far as the river.
sentences.s097-s18: It was said that the earth became a moving sky; from Indian swords there kept falling (a rain of steel).
sentences.s097-s19: News reached Iran one by one: that a buyer of the lofty throne had come.
sentences.s097-s20: They turned their faces toward Zabulistan; the world became entirely full of talk.
sentences.s097-s29: Now the mighty one's mane turned into a hoop; the Kabuli dagger could not bend it.
sentences.s097-s30: Now Rostam became like a dark cypress; it became fitting for him to don a moon-like cap.
sentences.s097-s31: He needs one war-steed; from these Arabian horses that will not do.
sentences.s097-s32: I will search once in Pilten; I will inquire from every side where the assembly is.
sentences.s097-s5: News reached the Turks that he had passed away, so that the throne became vacant.
sentences.s097-s6: Afrasiab came to Khwar and Rey; he spared the world and let the water be.
sentences.s097-s7: Not a single person brought greetings for Pashang; his head was full of hatred and his heart full of war.
sentences.s097-s8: His heart itself had turned away from the throne and the head; he was steeped in the care of Aghrirath.
sentences.s097-s13: There can be no dealings between you and me forever; in the presence of your favor there is no way to meet.
sentences.s097-s14: Ears rang with that news: the imperial throne had become vacant.
sentences.s097-s15: A message came, delivered like a stone, to Afrasiab from the valiant Pashang.
sentences.s097-s16: That he should let loose the armies of Jihun and Barkash; do not hinder them until someone sits (in place) at the appointed time.
sentences.s097-s33: I will relate this tale to Rostam — that you are of the same mind about this matter.
sentences.s097-s34: that, out of hatred, you place (or set) Zādsham’s offspring between (them) and do not be sullen.
sentences.s097-s35: All the towns of Iran, from his words, were glad at heart and fresh‑faced.
sentences.s097-s36: From every quarter savage beasts and daring raiders charged, and he raised/formed armed war‑horsemen.
sentences.s097-s37: He said to Rostam thus: "O Piltan, your head towers above the assembly."
sentences.s097-s38: There lies a task ahead and a long hardship that will break your sleep, your rest, and your indulgence.
sentences.s097-s39: You are still wholly a babe; there is no time for battle — what can I do, since this is not the hour for feasting?
sentences.s097-s40: Even now milk is on your lips; your heart still seeks caressing and joy.
sentences.s097-s1: He had a son — a certain one who fulfilled his desire; the father had made him, his name was Garshasp.
sentences.s097-s2: He came and sat upon the throne, and at times placed the Kayanian crown upon his head.
sentences.s097-s3: When he sat upon the throne, he would sometimes hold the world with beauty and royal glory.
sentences.s097-s4: Thus it continued until this time arose; the tree of calamity brought forth a burden of hatred.
sentences.s097-s41: How can I send you to the plain of battle, before the Turks who are full of hatred and sorrow?
sentences.s097-s42: What will you say, what will you devise, what will you answer? — May your spouse be moonlike and fair.
sentences.s097-s43: Thus Rostam said to Sam (to the hands of Sam): 'I am not a man of quiet and the cup.'
sentences.s097-s44: Such a mane and these long claws were not meant to be reared with pampering.
sentences.s097-s9: Pashang never turned his face to him; that bright sword became full of dark rust.
sentences.s097-s10: A messenger went up to him; for a year and a month he showed him no face.
sentences.s097-s11: He kept saying, 'If you abandon the leadership of the throne and make Aghrirath his fitting companion, you will do wrong.'
sentences.s097-s12: You will spill your brother’s blood; you will even flee from a tamed bird.
sentences.s097-s25: Thus spoke then the famed Zāl-e-Zar: that when I girded my waist to a man.
sentences.s097-s26: When a horseman like me set foot upon the saddle, no one took away my sword and mace.
sentences.s097-s27: To the very place where I pressed my foot, the reins of the horsemen became (loosened) once/thereupon.
sentences.s097-s28: Day and night in battle I was the same; from old age, year by year I became fearful.
sentences.s097-s21: They spoke to Zāl rather bluntly, saying that you have grasped the world too easily in your fist.
sentences.s097-s22: From Sām until you became a champion, we did not have a single bright (cheerful) day.
sentences.s097-s23: An army moved from the Jeyhun to this side, so that the sun vanished from the world.
sentences.s097-s24: If you know a remedy for this, devise it, for the spahbed has come into dire straits.
sentences.s097-s49: Once I must, like a tall mountain, lie in the bend of a snare.
sentences.s097-s50: Sometimes I will take up a mace like a solitary bare mountain when a troop from Turan comes before me.
sentences.s097-s51: I will strike their leaders with that mace; no quarrelsome man will come against me.
sentences.s097-s52: so that I leave the surface of the earth without an army, so that clouds rain blood upon the battlefield.
sentences.s097-s45: If the plain of vengeance comes and the fighting is fierce, my ally is God‑blessed (fortunate).
sentences.s097-s46: You will see how I am in battle — how I become when blood begins to pour.
sentences.s097-s47: I have a certain cloud in my grip inside (i.e. within my grasp) that is the color of water and whose rain is blood.
sentences.s097-s48: It continually kindles fire from its core; it continually gnaws at (or wears away) the elephants' brains and their heads.
